schools.summary: The 'third year' is reckoned from Zarephath, not the drought's start; Luke 4:25 and James 5:17 fix the whole span at three years and six months.
Clarke reads 'the earth' as haadamah, the land — the drought probably reached no further than Israel.
Gill dates the command near the fourth year of famine; God bids Elijah show himself only when the hour to favour Israel has come.
Henry: it bodes well for a people when God calls his ministers from their corners — a sign he will give rain.

themes: the word of the Lord to Elijah

schools.summary: Gill: the going showed Elijah's cheerful obedience and great courage in facing a king who sought his life.
Keil and Delitzsch let the dropped clause govern all — a 'sore famine in Samaria,' the hunger reaching the palace itself.
Henry: long shut up at Cherith and Zarephath, Elijah waited on the command and now walks straight toward his hunter.

themes: faith that fears God more than kings

schools.summary: Gill: Obadiah the governor of the house, whom the Jews would identify with Obadiah the prophet.
Clarke: 'Obadiah feared the Lord greatly' — a sincere worshipper whose conduct proved both piety and humanity.
Ellicott: the name 'servant of Jehovah' fits the man and exposes Ahab's temporising — a Jehovah-worshipper high in a Baal court.
Henry marvels at grace in a hostile place: God has his remnant among all sorts — saints in Nero's household and in Ahab's.
Gill: the slain were prophets from the schools, teachers of the people though not workers of miracles.
Clarke: 'bread and water' signify the necessaries of life, all a hunted remnant could be kept alive on.
Ellicott: the first clash of royal power with the prophetic order, known only by this allusion; Elijah's 'I alone remain' sits in tension with the hidden hundred.
Henry: Obadiah did not think it enough to fear God himself but sheltered and fed those who feared him.
Gill and Clarke on the ground: fountains, brooks, and 'marshy or well-watered districts' where grass might survive.
Henry: Ahab took pains to seek grass but none to seek God's favour — fencing against the effect, not removing the cause.
The distinction from Judah, where no drought is named, shows the ground of God's controversy (Hosea 11:12; Amos 4:7-8).

themes: caring for beasts not the soul

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the two greatest men in the realm split the land and tramp it alone — the measure of the famine's severity.
Gill gives only the plain sense: one took one part, the other the other.
Henry: the king's grass-hunt is the providential road by which Obadiah is steered straight to the waiting prophet.
Ellicott: Obadiah's servile, fearful courtesy stands in dignified contrast with Elijah's brief 'Behold, Elijah!'
Henry: the reverence of a son to the father of the prophets — proof that Obadiah feared God greatly.

themes: the meeting of steward and prophet

schools.summary: Gill: Elijah desired no concealment; his orders were to show himself, and Obadiah was one of Ahab's servants.
Henry: Elijah transfers the title 'lord' to the king — a prophet seeks not honour from men — and sends word ahead openly.
Keil and Delitzsch: the plain command lands on a man who at once measures what it may cost him.
Gill: Obadiah asks in what he has offended God or his prophet, that so deadly an errand should fall to him.
Henry: not the protest of a coward but of a faithful servant who has already risked much and cannot understand being exposed.
Keil and Delitzsch hear a hyperbole 'suggested by inward excitement and fear' in the sweep 'every kingdom and every nation.'
Clarke: the search was Ahab's own, argument of a king with real reach over neighbouring nations.
Gill: the oath was the form chosen to ascertain the truth of what Obadiah was about to say.

debates.question: Whose relentless manhunt was it — Ahab's or Jezebel's?

sides.position: Ahab's own, proof of his power over neighbouring kingdoms
Jezebel's malice working in Ahab's name, connived at by his timidity (as with Naboth)

schools.summary: Gill: the message, if it failed of proof, 'would be of fatal consequence' — his fear plain in what he says next.
Keil and Delitzsch place the clause exactly: after so deadly a hunt, the quarry calmly bids his hunter's servant announce him.
Ellicott hears a survival of the physical sense of 'Spirit' — the whirlwind, the breath of the Lord (2 Kings 2:16; Acts 8:39).
Clarke: Obadiah supposed the Spirit had hidden Elijah through the whole drought and would sweep him off again at the first danger.
Gill: small raptures may already have been known to Obadiah, so he feared Elijah would vanish.
Henry: Obadiah misjudged the prophet's intent, suspecting he meant only to expose Ahab's malice; he pleads his lifelong fear of God.
Gill: he pleads his deed not in ostentation but to show it would be ungrateful to sacrifice such a friend at court.
Keil and Delitzsch: the plea establishes that though he wears Ahab's livery he is not of Ahab's party.
Clarke dates the persecution to the dearth, when Jezebel, unable to find Elijah, wreaked her vengeance on the prophets within reach.
Gill: he dreads that if Elijah is removed and not found, the king's rage will fall on the messenger.
Keil and Delitzsch: the closing beat of one anxious argument — stripped of all reasoning, the fear reduces to 'he shall slay me.'
Henry: tyrants in their passion are often unreasonably outrageous even toward their own confidants.
Gill: the oath assures Obadiah that Elijah would certainly be found and not expose him to danger.
Keil and Delitzsch: he swears by 'the Lord of hosts, before whom I stand,' and on that word Obadiah goes to the king.
Henry: he swears by the Lord of hosts, who has all power and can protect his servants against hell and earth.
Gill: on the prophet's oath Obadiah was satisfied and delivered the message without fear.
Keil and Delitzsch: the narrator's hinge — the king who could never find Elijah now walks out to a meeting the prophet arranged.
Henry: a guilty king drawn against his will to the prophet he loathes — hating and fearing him, as Herod did John.
The Hebrew 'ocher' (troubler) recalls Achan (Genesis 34:30); Ellicott hears defiance flung in the very land Ahab troubled.
Henry: men betray their hearts by their words — 'my lord Elijah' from a God-fearing steward, 'thou troubler' from a God-forsaking king (Acts 17:6).
Clarke: the cause of the dearth is set on its true ground — king and people forsook God, who shut up the heavens; Elijah was only the minister.
Ellicott: 'the Baalim,' a contemptuous plural for false gods generally; Keil makes the guilt dynastic — Ahab and his father's house.
Henry: Ahab is the Achan; nothing troubles a land more than the impiety of princes and their families.
Ellicott expounds Carmel — a garden-ridge, El Maharrakah 'the burning,' an old high place (Joshua 19:26; Isaiah 33:9); the 400 groves never reappear.
Clarke: two royal establishments — 450 priests of Baal (the sun), 400 of Asherah/Astarte who ate at Jezebel's table.
Gill: Carmel was chosen for a public trial, away from Jezebel and within sight of the coming rain.

themes: the prophets of Baal and the groves

schools.summary: Gill: the summons went out by messengers to the chief and principal men of Israel.
Henry: Ahab obeyed either from fear of Elijah or hope of rain; God made the synagogue of Satan come and worship at his feet (Revelation 3:9).
Keil: 'limp upon both sides' — the law demands a whole heart (Deuteronomy 6:4-5; Psalms 119:113); Ellicott calls the question the motto of Elijah's life, listened to in awestruck silence.
Clarke: 'hop about upon two boughs' like a restless bird — dreading Jehovah yet fearing king and queen.
Henry: they worshipped God to please the prophets and Baal to please the court; 'he that is not with God is against him' (Joshua 24:15).
Gill: he was the only prophet present, 'at least as he thought,' the rest supposed slain.
Clarke: the queen's 400 were not present — the same court prophets Ahab consults before Ramoth-gilead (1 Kings 22:6).
Keil and Delitzsch: the very inequality makes the test fair; the proposal rests on the fire of Leviticus 9:24.

themes: one against four hundred and fifty

schools.summary: Gill: being many and backed by the king, Baal's prophets could well bear the cost of the bullocks.
Keil and Delitzsch lay out the structure: two victims identically dressed, 'put no fire under,' the God that answers by fire to be God.
Clarke: Baal being the sun, the god of fire, Elijah stakes the test on the idol's own element and grants every advantage.
Ellicott: a sign from heaven (Leviticus 9:24; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1), not to be demanded (Matthew 12:38-39) but granted when needed, reclaiming Nature's sphere for the Lord.
Henry: fire not water, for atonement by sacrifice must precede mercy — he that can give fire can give rain.

themes: the God that answers by fire
the sign aimed at the idol's boast

schools.summary: Clarke: 'for ye are many' — the greater number given the advantage of going first.
Gill canvasses why they agreed: belief that Baal (Mars or the sun) commanded fire, or plain necessity, fearing the people would stone them.
Henry: Elijah gives them the lead 'for their greater confusion,' guarding against fraud with 'put no fire under.'
Clarke: they leaped up and down at the altar in wild dances and cries, a spectacle he likens to heathen priests he had watched.
Ellicott: the frenzied 'O Baal, hear us' contrasts with Elijah's solemn prayer — the heathen think to be heard for much speaking (Matthew 6:7); grave irony in the blank silence.
Henry: 450 crying as one, louder and longer than Diana's worshippers (Acts 19:34), senseless in their address to Baal.

Keil: the sting is 'for he is a god' set against all-too-human excuses; Ellicott calls it merely the bitter irony of contempt, like the scorn of the psalmists (Psalms 115:4-8; Isaiah 44:9-20), not Paul's courtesy at Athens (Acts 17:22-23).
Clarke unpacks each clause — revery, hunting, a journey, and Jarchi's coarsest reading — noting Asiatic gods that sleep, as Vishnu four months a year.
Henry: idolatry deserves to be exposed to scorn, yet this cannot justify ridiculing the true worship of God in Christ.

debates.question: How much detail should be pressed from Elijah's mockery?

sides.position: Each clause names a supposed action of Baal — worth expounding fully
It is merely undifferentiated contempt; over-ingenuity to itemize it

outliers.note: Piles on comparative-religion detail — Rabbi Jarchi's coarse gloss and the sleeping gods Vishnu and Budhoo — which Ellicott judges 'over-ingenuity.'

themes: the irony of 'he is a god'

schools.summary: Clarke: the self-gashing was the rite of a barbarous religion, common to Syrians, Persians, Greeks, and Indians alike.
Ellicott: 'lancets' should be 'lances'; the self-mutilation was perhaps a survival of human sacrifice, the delusion that self-torture wins favour.
Henry: God never required such honour — the law forbids cutting oneself (Deuteronomy 14:1); the devil's service is cruel to the body.
Clarke: 'prophesied' means made supplication (naba), as when Saul was 'among the prophets' (1 Samuel 10:5).

Ellicott: they raved in frenzy like Saul in madness (1 Samuel 18:10; 19:20-24); true inspiration gives no place to frenzy — 'the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets' (1 Corinthians 14:32).
Henry: idols can do neither good nor evil; God would not suffer the devil to send fire (Revelation 13:13-14) since the trial was by consent of parties.
Keil and Delitzsch: an altar of Jehovah already stood on Carmel; Ellicott sees the exclusive Jerusalem sanctuary broken down by the schism.
Clarke: the altar of Carmel was famous even among the heathen — Tacitus and Suetonius record Vespasian consulting it.
Henry: Elijah revives the fathers' God, not a new religion; where reformation cannot go the whole way, do what you can.

themes: repairing the altar of the Lord

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the twelve stones declare that the division into two kingdoms is at variance with Israel's calling (Genesis 32:29; 35:10); Ellicott: the tribes are still one in the covenant.
Henry: though ten tribes revolted, Elijah owns them all as God's; he must now wrestle like Jacob and be a prince with God (Hosea 12:4).

themes: Israel the name of the whole people

schools.summary: Gill: the altar built in the name of the Lord whom the twelve tribes, now divided, ought still to worship as one.
Ellicott weighs the 'measure' (a third of an ephah) and prefers a trench 'large enough for sowing two measures of seed'; Clarke: the trench was to detain the poured water.

themes: the altar in the name of the Lord

schools.summary: Clarke: the drenching prevents any suspicion of concealed fire (heathen altars had hidden funnels); water would extinguish it, putting the miracle beyond suspicion.

Henry: 'where is the fire? God will provide' (Genesis 22:7-8); though Elijah was no priest and Carmel no temple, the sacrifice was accepted — the Levitical rule a type to be fulfilled (Colossians 2:22; Hebrews 8:13).
Gill: each time four more barrels were poured upon the wood.
Clarke's reasoning governs: since one soaking would quench any hidden fire, three make fraud inconceivable — twelve barrels leaving no crevice dry.
The calm, threefold command throws Elijah's composure into relief against Baal's frenzy, courting the strictest scrutiny.
Gill: so large an effusion that the water ran down and stood in the channel.
Ellicott: the water was drawn from a perennial spring near the traditional scene (with Josephus), never failing even in drought — not carried from the distant sea.
The lavish spending of water in a three-year drought makes the coming fire the more unanswerable.
Clarke: the patriarchs are named so the people would know the answering God is the God of their fathers, and turn back to the old religion.
Ellicott: 'Jehovah' the self-existent joined with the covenant name; Elijah desires to efface himself, a voice preparing the way of the Lord.
Henry: he chose the evening-sacrifice hour to testify communion with Jerusalem; two pleas — God's glory (Numbers 16:28-29) and the people's edification.

themes: the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel
glory of God and turning of hearts

schools.summary: Gill: the doubled 'hear me' expresses importunity and the vehement desire of his soul for what concerns God's glory.
Keil and Delitzsch: the miracle would accredit Elijah and prove Jehovah the living God against the dumb idol.
Henry: the deepest plea — that God has 'turned their heart back again'; the fire is sought for repentance, not spectacle.

themes: the turning of the heart as God's gift

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: not lightning but miraculous fire from heaven (Leviticus 9:24; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1), burning stones, dust, and water.
Clarke numbers the stages and marks the wonder against nature — the fire acted downward, removing all suspicion; Pindar's Rhodian tale a pagan echo.
Henry: the flame licked up the water 'in order to the intended rain' (Psalms 135:7), and consumed the very altar, never to be used again.
Gill: they fell down astonished and ashamed of their idolatry.
Clarke urges the covenant name be heard — 'Jehovah, He is the God! Baal is not the God.'
Ellicott: exactly as at the tabernacle's inauguration (Leviticus 9:24), with the added cry that He alone is God.
Henry: an agreed verdict, yet many were convinced only, not converted; blessed are they who believe without seeing (John 20:29).
Keil and Delitzsch: not revenge but the theocracy's law that idolatry and false prophets be destroyed (Deuteronomy 13:6-18; 17:2-7).
Clarke: their lives were forfeit under that law, and Ahab, present, consented.
Henry: Elijah acts by an extraordinary commission not to be drawn into precedent; the 400 groves escaped to become Ahab's ruin (1 Kings 22:6).

debates.question: How should a Christian regard the slaughter of Baal's prophets?

sides.position: A righteous execution under a divinely given law
A fierce act of the old Law, fitted to hard hearts and not to be imitated — quoting such cases is 'a spiritual anachronism' (Luke 9:54)

outliers.note: Calls the frequent Christian appeal to such examples 'a spiritual anachronism,' distinguishing the old Law's severity from gospel mercy.

themes: zeal and the ethics of judgment

schools.summary: Keil: the curse's cause removed, the curse could go — yet only by the prophet's word; Ellicott hears a touch of scorn, the king to revel, the prophet to pray.
Clarke: the invitation shows Ahab and Elijah now on good terms, a proof the priests were slain with the king's consent.
Henry: Elijah 'by faith hears the sound of abundance of rain'; rain is the river of God (Psalms 65:9), yet must be asked (Zechariah 10:1; Ecclesiastes 9:7).

themes: the sound of abundance of rain
the feasting king and the praying prophet

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Elijah prays for the promised rain, the pattern of effectual prayer (James 5:18); Ellicott notes the peculiar posture, still seen among dervishes.
Clarke: he knelt and bowed so his forehead touched the ground, face between his knees.
Henry: he withdrew to the private top of Carmel (Amos 9:3), set himself on his watch-tower (Habakkuk 2:1); public servants must be private with God.
Clarke and Keil fix the vantage — the sea full in view from Carmel; Ellicott makes the delay proverbial for persevering prayer (James 5:17), a lesson of patience to Elijah.
Henry: six times the servant sees nothing, yet Elijah holds on like Jacob — 'I will not let thee go except thou bless me' (Genesis 32:26).
Clarke: 'like the hollow of a man's hand'; he had seen such a cloud at sea before a storm, and Bruce a whirling one in Abyssinia that recalled Elijah.
Keil and Delitzsch: the prompt charge to harness and descend before the downpour turns the dusty plain to mud.
Henry: great blessings arise from small beginnings — never despise the day of small things (Zechariah 4:10); so too the Spirit's first workings in the heart.

themes: a cloud like a man's hand

schools.summary: Keil: 'hither and thither' — very speedily the heaven blackened and the great rain fell; Ellicott: the first mention of Jezreel ('Jehovah hath sown') as a royal city (Joshua 19:18).
Henry: not a passing morning cloud but a plentiful rain and an earnest of more (Psalms 68:9) — mercy following hard on reformation.
Keil reads the running as humility to win the king and fortify him against Jezebel; Ellicott sees enthusiastic thankfulness, natural in the warrior of God though strange for a Samuel or Isaiah.
Clarke: not a herald chanting praises, but 'the spirit of strength' upon him, girding his loins to outrun the chariot and show he was under divine inspiration.
Gill, with the Targum: 'the spirit of strength from the Lord was with him.'
Henry: Ahab never took him into the chariot, so Elijah ran as a footman — ministers should not affect worldly grandeur.

debates.question: What does Elijah's running before Ahab chiefly signify?

sides.position: Divine strength and inspiration, that the king might respect and fear him
Humility and faithful service, to touch the king's heart and fortify him against Jezebel
An enthusiastic reaction of thankfulness after his stern calm

themes: power and humility in one figure
the servant of the Lord of hosts
